name: Chun
text: @bitschmidty Bitcoin has far more than just four known vulnerabilities. These four issues addressed by BIP-54 are, in my view, relatively trivial historical edge cases. I don't believe they are significant enough to warrant the coordination cost, node upgrade effort, and community attention

name: Chun
text: @darosior @A86664949 Not just that. The current proposed fix for timewarp is ugly. I don't like that it only targets difficulty-changing blocks.

name: Chun
text: @A86664949 Timewarp attack: This issue has been known for a long. However, it doesn't provide significant benefit to a miner because you cannot predict who will mine the next block. The only practical use case is disrupting certain altcoins. Since it brings little real advantage to miners

name: my
text: I'll be proposing in the coming weeks an alt to BIP-54 which does not eliminate 64 byte transactions and may also offer some other benefits to SPV clients. I'm not surprised that BIP-0054 went FINAL, but I'd like to register my dissent publicly as it seems the authors are

name: Luke Dashjr
text: @hampus_s Indeed, BIP54's "let's fix a non-issue that wouldn't have even been a risk for 53 years, is impossible to exploit, and probably will be entirely impossible by the key date in 2079 - by permanently harming mining scalability" is just stupid.

name: jamesob
text: Hang on are we talking about QC, GCC, or 110?

name: /dev/fd0
text: Yes it will be noticed publicly. But then why are we trying to fix it with BIP 54?

name: M. Friedenbach 🏴‍☠️
text: @JeremyRubin Jeremy, please include an alternative to the time-warp fix in BIP 54, one that doesn't cut off forward blocks as an upgrade mechanism. It would be sufficient to limit the relative time-warp from the last block period rather than capping absolute time-warp.

name: floppy.md
text: @TheBlueMatt @tierotiero @brian_trollz @satofishi Still don't understand why SIGHASH_SINGLE bug is not being fixed in this consensus cleanup
